narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 40.3% chance that you would fail it. This probability crossed our threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with the strongest projected odds of bar success to maintain high pass rates. The model's decision hinged on just a few key aspects of your profile, with your undergraduate GPA, Year 3 decile ranking, and LSAT score standing out as the most influential. Your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50—higher than the 3.24 average among students who passed the bar—strongly worked in your favor, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by showing you have the academic foundation to succeed. In contrast, your Year 3 decile of 1 was the biggest drag, as it signaled lower performance relative to peers that year and pulled the prediction toward failure, unlike the typical 5.91 decile for bar passers. Your LSAT score of 30 also nudged things slightly toward a higher failure risk, falling well below the 37.27 average of those who passed. While your middle family income bracket aligns with over a third of successful students and your choice to study full-time matches nearly all of them, these didn't outweigh the concerns from your rankings and test score. Compared to admitted students, who generally show stronger decile placements and higher LSATs alongside any GPA, your profile appeared riskier overall despite the GPA strength. This isn't a judgment on your potential or hard work—it's a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, as that could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening recent academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate upward momentum. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas, as many students improve their profiles and succeed on later attempts.
